stem: A 28-year-old non-pregnant woman presents to her GP with 24 hours of dysuria, urinary frequency and suprapubic discomfort. She has had two similar episodes in the past year both resolved with antibiotics. She has no fever, no flank pain and no vaginal discharge. She is not sexually active in the past 4 weeks. She has no penicillin allergy and no recent antibiotic use. Urinalysis is positive for leukocyte esterase and nitrites; midstream urine has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 76, RR 14, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness, no renal angle tenderness, no peritonism, no vaginal discharge
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis - leukocytes 3+, nitrites positive, blood trace, glucose negative; urine pregnancy test negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic?

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose as empirical therapy covering atypical urinary pathogens
options.rationale: Macrolides do not treat the bacteriuria of cystitis (E. coli is the predominant pathogen); azithromycin is not appropriate cystitis therapy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute cystitis)

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 3 days as effective broad-spectrum first-line cystitis therapy
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones are reserved for failure or resistance to first-line agents due to collateral resistance and adverse effects; they are not first-line for uncomplicated cystitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute cystitis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg twice daily for 5 days as broad cover for uncomplicated cystitis
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate has high E. coli resistance and is not first-line; it is reserved for complicated infection or specific resistance patterns. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute cystitis)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days as a broader cephalosporin cystitis option
options.rationale: Cefalexin is acceptable second-line but trimethoprim is preferred first-line in non-pregnant women without contraindication. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute cystitis)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg orally each night for 3 days as empirical therapy for uncomplicated cystitis
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines list trimethoprim 300 mg orally at night for 3 days as first-line for uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women without recent trimethoprim use or culture-confirmed resistance. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute cystitis)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women in Australia is treated with trimethoprim 300 mg orally at night for 3 days, or nitrofurantoin 100 mg orally 6-hourly for 5 days, with cefalexin as a second-line option. Fluoroquinolones are reserved due to resistance and adverse effects. Pregnancy, immunosuppression, flank pain or systemic symptoms shift the decision to complicated UTI pathways.
key_points: Trimethoprim 300 mg nocte for 3 days is first-line for uncomplicated cystitis
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg 6-hourly for 5 days is an equivalent first-line option
Fluoroquinolones are reserved for resistance or failure of first-line therapy
explanation.pearls: Recurrent cystitis (3 or more episodes per year) warrants behavioural advice and, in selected cases, post-coital or continuous low-dose prophylaxis; non-antibiotic options include topical oestrogen in postmenopausal women.

stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.2%) presents to ED with 36 hours of right leg pain following a minor abrasion. The pain is out of proportion to the visible skin changes. There is firm induration extending beyond the visible erythema and a small area of grey discolouration with a foul-smelling exudate. Blood pressure has fallen from 134/80 in triage to 92/58 mmHg after analgesia. He is alert. Bedside blood tests have returned.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius, HR 122, RR 26, BP 92/58 mmHg, SpO2 97% in room air, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Right calf erythema with central grey patch, woody induration extending proximally, severe tenderness on palpation, palpable peripheral pulses, no crepitus on examination
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 x10^9/L, Hb 124 g/L, Na 130 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L, glucose 16 mmol/L, CRP 220 mg/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Activate emergency surgical referral for theatre and commence intravenous meropenem with clindamycin
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, woody induration, skin discolouration, sodium below 135 mmol/L and CRP above 150 mg/L give a LRINEC score consistent with necrotising fasciitis; emergent surgical debridement is the cornerstone of treatment alongside broad-spectrum antibiotics including a toxin suppressor (clindamycin or lincomycin). (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Admit for IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly and review with the surgical team in the morning round
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis requires same-hour surgical debridement; delay until morning rounds increases mortality. Flucloxacillin alone does not cover Gram-negative or anaerobic streptococcal toxin production. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Arrange contrast-enhanced CT of the right leg before contacting surgery to confirm fascial involvement
options.rationale: Imaging may support diagnosis but should not delay surgical exploration when clinical features and biochemistry strongly suggest necrotising fasciitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Admit to general medical unit for IV vancomycin and serial wound photographs to assess progression
options.rationale: Medical management alone is inadequate for necrotising fasciitis; mortality without prompt surgical debridement is around 70 to 80 percent. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections

options.text: Commence intravenous fluid bolus only, then admit to the diabetic foot service for podiatry review
options.rationale: Septic shock with limb signs requires immediate surgical and antimicrobial therapy; admission to podiatry-led pathways without acute surgery loses critical hours. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Pain disproportionate to examination, woody induration beyond erythema, skin discolouration or anaesthesia, plus systemic toxicity should prompt urgent surgical debridement. Broad-spectrum antibiotics include carbapenem or piperacillin-tazobactam plus a protein synthesis inhibitor (clindamycin or lincomycin) to suppress streptococcal toxin production. Mortality rises sharply with each hour of delay to debridement.
key_points: Pain out of proportion plus woody induration plus systemic toxicity = surgical emergency
LRINEC supports the diagnosis but absence does not exclude necrotising fasciitis
Add a toxin suppressor (clindamycin or lincomycin) to broad antibiotic cover
explanation.pearls: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is adjunctive in some Australian centres for clostridial myonecrosis but does not replace emergent surgical debridement and broad antimicrobial therapy.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: Necrotising soft tissue infections
ANZ Burns and Plastic Surgery Network: Necrotising fasciitis

stem: A 38-year-old previously well man presents to ED with 12 hours of fever, severe headache, photophobia and neck stiffness. He is drowsy but obeying commands. There is no rash. He has no penicillin allergy and no recent overseas travel. CT brain is unremarkable. You are preparing to perform a lumbar puncture but blood cultures are not yet drawn. The bedside team is asking about the order of interventions.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.1 degrees Celsius, HR 108, RR 22, BP 122/76 mmHg, SpO2 98% in room air, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Neck stiffness, photophobia, no rash, no focal neurology, pupils equal and reactive, no papilloedema, alert but drowsy
data_table.Investigations: CT brain no mass lesion no haemorrhage no hydrocephalus, WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 240 mg/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, glucose 6.8 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate sequence of interventions?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV first then blood cultures then dexamethasone then LP if no focal neurology develops
options.rationale: Blood cultures should be drawn before antibiotics where possible (within minutes) and dexamethasone is given with or just before antibiotics for maximum benefit. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial meningitis)

options.text: Lumbar puncture first then dexamethasone then ceftriaxone after CSF is in the lab and Gram stain returned
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay while awaiting CSF Gram stain increases mortality; antibiotics and dexamethasone should not wait for LP results. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial meningitis)

options.text: Blood cultures then dexamethasone 10 mg IV then ceftriaxone 2 g IV then lumbar puncture as feasible
options.rationale: Australian guidance for adult bacterial meningitis is blood cultures first, dexamethasone 10 mg IV just before or with the first antibiotic dose, ceftriaxone 2 g IV 12-hourly empirically, and LP performed as soon as safely possible after antibiotics are given. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial meningitis)

options.text: Vancomycin and meropenem with dexamethasone after MRI brain has excluded an epidural collection
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added only if pneumococcal resistance is suspected; meropenem is for penicillin allergy or atypical history; MRI is not the gating step before antibiotics. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial meningitis)

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IM in the resuscitation area without further CT or LP because of suspected meningococcal disease
options.rationale: IM penicillin is used pre-hospital when transit will delay IV access; in ED with IV access, IV ceftriaxone is preferred and CT is already done. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Bacterial meningitis)

explanation.summary: Adult bacterial meningitis suspected on clinical grounds is treated with empirical ceftriaxone 2 g IV 12-hourly, with dexamethasone 10 mg IV given just before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce neurological sequelae especially in pneumococcal disease. Blood cultures are drawn before antibiotics where feasible; lumbar puncture follows when safe. Add benzylpenicillin or ampicillin to cover Listeria in those over 50 years, pregnant or immunocompromised.
key_points: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV given just before/with first antibiotic dose reduces neuro sequelae
Empirical ceftriaxone 2 g IV 12-hourly; add ampicillin for Listeria risk
Antibiotic delay while awaiting LP or imaging increases mortality
explanation.pearls: Indications for CT before LP include focal neurology, GCS under 12, immunocompromise, papilloedema or seizure within 7 days; in their absence, LP can proceed without CT but antibiotics still come first if LP is delayed.

stem: A 27-year-old man presents to a sexual health clinic with 10 days of fever, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy and a non-pruritic maculopapular rash on his trunk. He had unprotected receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 3 weeks ago. He has no past HIV testing history. He is otherwise well. He asks for the most accurate test for acute HIV at this stage of illness. Examination is consistent with mononucleosis-like illness.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.4 degrees Celsius, HR 96, RR 16, BP 118/74 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, faint maculopapular rash on trunk, pharyngeal erythema without exudate, no oral candida, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.8 x10^9/L with lymphocyte 1.0 x10^9/L, ALT 78 U/L, monospot pending, EBV/CMV serology pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnostic test?

options.text: HIV RNA PCR quantitative assay to identify acute infection ahead of antibody seroconversion
options.rationale: During acute HIV seroconversion, viral RNA appears around 10 days post-exposure, before HIV antibody seroconversion (typically 3 to 6 weeks); HIV RNA is the test of choice when acute HIV is suspected. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Fourth-generation HIV antibody-antigen combination test alone with retest at 6 weeks if negative
options.rationale: Fourth-generation tests detect p24 antigen and antibodies and have shorter window than antibody-only tests, but HIV RNA is still more sensitive in the very early acute window. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Western blot HIV antibody confirmation as the gold standard for diagnosis of acute infection presentation
options.rationale: Western blot relies on antibody seroconversion (3 to 6 weeks) and is often negative during acute infection; it is no longer first-line confirmation in Australian practice. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: CD4 lymphocyte subset count alone as the most sensitive test for acute HIV at this stage of illness
options.rationale: CD4 count is used for staging after diagnosis and prognostic monitoring; it is not a diagnostic test for acute HIV. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

options.text: Rapid point-of-care HIV antibody finger-prick test as the most appropriate initial test in this setting
options.rationale: Point-of-care antibody tests have longer window periods than laboratory fourth-generation or RNA testing and may miss acute infection. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion typically presents 2 to 4 weeks after exposure with a mononucleosis-like illness. HIV RNA PCR is detectable from around 10 days, before fourth-generation antibody-antigen tests, and well before antibody-only or Western blot. Australian sexual health practice combines both fourth-generation serology and HIV RNA when acute HIV is suspected to maximise early detection. Post-test discussion, contact tracing and prompt antiretroviral initiation follow confirmed diagnosis.
key_points: HIV RNA PCR is positive earliest in acute infection (around 10 days post-exposure)
Fourth-generation antibody-antigen tests detect p24 and antibodies, window 2 to 3 weeks
Western blot relies on antibody and is often negative in acute infection
explanation.pearls: Detecting acute HIV is high-yield - viral load is highest in primary infection, so identifying and starting antiretroviral therapy markedly reduces onward transmission and may preserve immune function.

stem: A 34-year-old man recently arrived from Vietnam attends a refugee health clinic for screening. He is asymptomatic. He had BCG vaccination in childhood. His QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is positive. CXR is normal. Sputum cannot be produced. He is not pregnant, not on immunosuppression and not living with HIV. ALT is normal. He has no household contacts of active TB and no other tuberculosis risk factors apart from being from a high-prevalence country.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 118/72 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air, BMI 22 kg/m^2
data_table.Examination: Well, no lymphadenopathy, chest clear, no scars apart from BCG, abdomen unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: QuantiFERON positive (interferon-gamma response above threshold); CXR no infiltrate, no cavitation, no hilar lymphadenopathy; ALT 28 U/L; HIV negative; hepatitis B and C negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure him that QuantiFERON positivity reflects prior BCG and arrange a repeat test in 2 years
options.rationale: QuantiFERON is not affected by BCG vaccination (the antigens are M. tuberculosis-specific ESAT-6 and CFP-10) - a positive result indicates latent infection in this context. (Source: Australian TB Standards)

options.text: Commence rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol four-drug therapy for 2 months then continue 4 months
options.rationale: RIPE is for active TB, not latent infection; the absence of symptoms, normal CXR and negative sputum exclude active disease. (Source: Australian TB Standards)

options.text: Commence isoniazid 300 mg daily with pyridoxine 25 mg for 6 to 9 months and monitor LFTs at baseline and at 4 weeks
options.rationale: Latent TB in a low-risk adult is treated with isoniazid 300 mg orally daily for 6 to 9 months plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily to prevent peripheral neuropathy; LFTs are checked at baseline and at 4 weeks, then if symptomatic. (Source: Australian TB Standards)

options.text: Refer for sputum induction and bronchoscopy to obtain microbiological samples before any treatment is considered
options.rationale: Sputum induction and bronchoscopy are reserved for suspected active pulmonary TB; he has normal CXR and is asymptomatic so they are not indicated. (Source: Australian TB Standards)

options.text: Commence rifampicin 600 mg daily monotherapy for 4 months as a shorter latent TB regimen for low-risk adults
options.rationale: Rifampicin 4 months is an acceptable shorter alternative for some patients, but isoniazid for 6 to 9 months remains the preferred Australian first-line and the patient has no contraindication to isoniazid. (Source: Australian TB Standards)

explanation.summary: Latent tuberculosis infection in an asymptomatic adult with positive QuantiFERON and normal CXR is treated with isoniazid 300 mg daily for 6 to 9 months plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily. LFTs are monitored at baseline and 4 weeks, then symptom-driven. Patient education about hepatotoxicity (anorexia, jaundice, dark urine) and the importance of completion is essential. BCG does not cause false-positive QuantiFERON results.
key_points: Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 6 to 9 months plus pyridoxine is AU first-line for LTBI
QuantiFERON uses M. tuberculosis-specific antigens and is not affected by BCG
Hepatotoxicity monitoring with baseline and 4-week LFTs, then symptom-driven
explanation.pearls: Three-month weekly rifapentine and isoniazid (3HP) directly observed is an effective shorter alternative now available in some Australian centres for selected patients with good follow-up.
